title: Name the reference: ratios, proportions, and percentages

objectives.statement: Interpret an ordered ratio in context, preserve its labels, and generate equivalent ratios.
Solve proportional relationships by identifying a constant multiplier and verify the solution by substitution.
Calculate a part, whole, or percentage while naming the denominator that defines 100 percent.
Distinguish percent change from percentage-point difference and challenge results with bounds or reverse calculations.

scenes.title: Two true percentages that answer different questions
scenes.contentHtml:
Two true percentages that answer different questions

A fictional archive marks 30 of 120 files for rechecking. Later it marks 36 of 120.

  • The marked share rose from 30/120=25%30/120=25\% to 36/120=30%36/120=30\%.
  • That is an increase of 5 percentage points.
  • Relative to the old marked share, the increase is (30%−25%)/25%=20%(30\%-25\%)/25\%=20\%.

Both 5 and 20 are correct, but they answer different questions. The denominator decides the meaning.

This lesson uses a reference-first method: label the compared quantities, name what represents one whole or the starting value, calculate, then reverse or bound the result.

Starting check

  1. Does the ratio 3 red : 5 blue mean the same as 5 red : 3 blue?
  2. In “18 is 30% of what number?”, which value represents 100%?
  3. Can a quantity increase by more than 100%?

If fractions are uncertain, read a/ba/b aloud as “a for every b” and attach the labels. If decimal arithmetic is the barrier, use exact fractions until the final conversion.

Source Note
claims claim-ratio-quotient, claim-fraction-dimensionlesssources source-nist-si-chapter8

NIST defines several fractions and ratios as quotients and quantities of dimension one. The archive and comparison are original.

Accessibility and alternatives

All comparisons appear in words and equations. No answer depends on color, a chart, dragging, or timing. In a nonvisual reading, say “marked files divided by all files” and name each numerator and denominator before the value.

scenes.title: Keep the ratio labels attached
scenes.contentHtml:
Keep the ratio labels attached

A ratio compares two quantities by division. Order matters:

red-to-blue=3 red5 blue≠5 red3 blue.\text{red-to-blue}=\frac{3\ \text{red}}{5\ \text{blue}}\neq\frac{5\ \text{red}}{3\ \text{blue}}.

Multiplying both terms by the same nonzero number creates an equivalent ratio: 3:5=6:103:5=6:10. The compared amounts change, but the quotient does not.

Definition
id definition-proportion

A proportion is an equation stating that two ratios are equal, such as 3/5=x/203/5=x/20.

Solve by following the multiplier: 5→205\to20 multiplies by 4, so 3→x3\to x also multiplies by 4 and x=12x=12. Cross multiplication encodes the same equality, but the multiplier exposes why the answer scales.

Part–whole percentages

“Percent” means per hundred. For a part PP of whole WW,

r=PW,p=100r,P=p100W.r=\frac{P}{W},\qquad p=100r,\qquad P=\frac{p}{100}W.

The whole WW is the reference that represents 100%. A result between 0% and 100% is expected only when 0≤P≤W0\le P\le W; ratios and percent changes can exceed 100% in other contexts.

Percent change

For old value O≠0O\ne0 and new value NN,

percent change=N−OO×100%.\text{percent change}=\frac{N-O}{O}\times100\%.

The denominator is the old value because the question asks how large the change is relative to the starting amount. Always report direction: increase or decrease.

Source Note
claims claim-ratio-quotient, claim-fraction-dimensionlesssources source-nist-si-chapter8

NIST's fraction definitions establish quotient structure and dimension-one results for like-quantity fractions. The proportion and percentage derivations are original elementary algebra.

scenes.title: Solve by following one multiplier
scenes.contentHtml:
Solve by following one multiplier

A fictional imaging queue processes 42 frames in 6 minutes at a constant rate. How many frames would it process in 15 minutes under that model?

Worked Example
id worked-example-frame-rate

1. Label the ratio. 42 frames/6 min42\text{ frames}/6\text{ min}.

2. State the assumption. The rate is constant, so frames are proportional to time.

3. Find the unit rate. 42/6=7 frames/min42/6=7\text{ frames/min}.

4. Scale. 7 frames/min×15 min=105 frames7\text{ frames/min}\times15\text{ min}=105\text{ frames}.

5. Verify. 105/15=7105/15=7, recovering the original quotient. Also, 15 minutes is 2.5 times 6 minutes, and 42×2.5=10542\times2.5=105.

6. Bound the claim. The result follows only under the stated constant-rate model; the arithmetic does not establish that a real queue remains constant.

Check
id check-proportional-assumption

If the first five minutes include a fixed startup delay, would direct proportionality necessarily remain appropriate? Explain.

No. A fixed offset can break the “same multiplier” structure. Proportionality is a model to justify, not a default triggered by two numbers.

Source Note
claims claim-ratio-quotientsources source-nist-si-chapter8

The worked example is original and applies quotient reasoning without claiming the fictional rate is empirical.

scenes.title: Percentage points are not percent change
scenes.contentHtml:
Percentage points are not percent change

Suppose a fictional pass fraction rises from 40% to 50%.

Misconception
id misconception-percent-change

Saying “it increased by 10%” silently treats the subtraction as a relative change. The direct subtraction is

50%−40%=10 percentage points.50\%-40\%=10\text{ percentage points}.

The percent increase uses the old 40% as reference:

50%−40%40%×100%=25%.\frac{50\%-40\%}{40\%}\times100\%=25\%.

A reverse check confirms it: 40%×1.25=50%40\%\times1.25=50\%.

The reference-first question is: “Ten is what fraction of the starting forty?” If no starting value is named, “percent change” is incomplete.

Source Note
claims claim-fraction-dimensionlesssources source-nist-si-chapter8

The distinction follows from which dimension-one quotient is formed. The example and repair are original.

scenes.title: Find the reference before calculating
scenes.contentHtml:
Find the reference before calculating

For each task, write labels → reference → equation → check.

1. Equivalent ratio

Complete 8:12=x:308:12=x:30.

Feedback: 12→3012\to30 multiplies by 2.5, so x=20x=20. Both ratios simplify to 2:32:3.

2. Find a part

What is 18% of 250?

Feedback: 0.18(250)=450.18(250)=45. The result is below the whole because the percentage is below 100%.

3. Find the whole

Thirty-six objects are 24% of a collection. Find the collection size.

Feedback: 36=0.24W36=0.24W, so W=150W=150. Reverse: 0.24(150)=360.24(150)=36.

4. Percent decrease

A fictional count falls from 80 to 62. Find the percentage decrease.

Feedback: The decrease is 18, referenced to 80: 18/80=0.225=22.5%18/80=0.225=22.5\%. Dividing by 62 would use the new value and answer a different question.

Recovery route

  • Ratio reversed: restore verbal labels before numbers.
  • Proportion gives an implausible direction: identify the multiplier on both quantities.
  • Part exceeds whole when percentage is below 100%: inspect multiplication versus division.
  • Percent-change denominator uncertain: write “change relative to starting value.”
  • Percentage points confused with percent: compute both and label them.
Source Note
claims claim-ratio-quotient, claim-fraction-dimensionlesssources source-nist-si-chapter8

All practice items, answers, and remediation are original applications of quotient relations.

scenes.title: Audit a changing mixture record
scenes.contentHtml:
Audit a changing mixture record

A fictional materials record describes 18 blue beads and 42 white beads. After 12 blue beads are added, no beads are removed.

Determine:

  1. the original blue-to-white ratio;
  2. the original blue percentage of all beads;
  3. the new blue percentage;
  4. the percentage-point change and relative percent change in the blue share.

Feedback after a complete attempt

Original ratio: 18:42=3:718:42=3:7. Original total: 60, so blue share is 18/60=30%18/60=30\%.

New blue count: 30; new total: 72. New blue share is 30/72=5/12≈41.67%30/72=5/12\approx41.67\%.

Percentage-point increase: 41.67−30=11.6741.67-30=11.67 points. Relative increase in the blue share:

41.67%−30%30%×100%≈38.89%.\frac{41.67\%-30\%}{30\%}\times100\%\approx38.89\%.

Check: a 38.89% increase applied to 30% gives about 41.67%. If you kept 60 as the new denominator, you failed to update the whole when objects were added.

Source Note
claims claim-ratio-quotient, claim-fraction-dimensionlesssources source-nist-si-chapter8

The bead record and all calculations are fictional original material.

scenes.title: Reconstruct the reference-first method
scenes.contentHtml:
Reconstruct the reference-first method

  1. Attach labels and preserve ratio order.
  2. State why proportional scaling is justified.
  3. Name the whole or starting value that supplies the denominator.
  4. Calculate with a fraction before converting to percent.
  5. Reverse, substitute, or bound the answer.

Retrieval

  • Why are 3:53:5 and 6:106:10 equivalent?
  • What assumption makes a proportion appropriate?
  • In percent change, why is the old value the denominator?
  • How do percentage points differ from relative percent change?

Then complete assessment.json. If ratio order fails, return to “Keep the ratio labels attached.” If the wrong denominator appears, redo the mixture transfer with every whole written explicitly. If arithmetic is correct but the comparison is unlabeled, add “percentage points” or “percent increase/decrease” before retrying.

Source Note
claims claim-ratio-quotient, claim-fraction-dimensionlesssources source-nist-si-chapter8

The synthesis uses the source's quotient framework; the reference-first procedure is original and makes no claim of measured effectiveness.

The digest covers discloseable material task instructions, not hidden system or provider instructions.

assessment.title: Ratios, proportions, and percentages mastery gate

items.prompt: A box has 12 large and 18 small objects. What is the large-to-small ratio in simplest form?

answer.reasoning: The requested order is large then small; divide both terms by 6.
The denominator is multiplied by 5, so the numerator must also be multiplied by 5.

items.prompt: Forty-two records are 28% of an archive. Find the archive size and show a reverse check.

answer.reasoning: The archive is the 100% reference, so divide the part by the decimal percentage.
acceptableResponses: 150 with an equivalent equation and reverse check

items.prompt: A count rises from 64 to 80. Enter the percent increase.

answer.reasoning: The change is 16 and the old-value reference is 64; 16/64 = 0.25.
commonErrors: Reporting the change of 16 as a percentage

items.prompt: A share falls from 50% to 35%. Report both the percentage-point decrease and the relative percent decrease, naming each reference.

answer.correct: The decrease is 15 percentage points. Relative to the starting 50%, it is 15/50 = 30%.
answer.summary: 15 percentage points and a 30% relative decrease.
answer.reasoning: Subtraction compares positions on the percentage scale; division by the starting share measures relative change.
acceptableResponses: 15 points; 30 percent decrease with the old 50 percent named

items.prompt: A fictional collection initially has 24 tagged and 56 untagged items. Sixteen tagged items are added and none removed. Find the initial tagged-to-untagged ratio, initial and new tagged percentages, percentage-point change, and relative percent change in tagged share. Show denominators and one reverse check.

answer.correct: Initial ratio 24:56 = 3:7. Initial share 24/80 = 30%. New share 40/96 = 41.666...%. Increase 11.666... percentage points; relative increase 11.666.../30 × 100 = 38.888...%. Check: 30% × 1.38888... = 41.666...%.
answer.summary: 3:7; 30%; about 41.67%; about 11.67 points; about 38.89% relative increase.
answer.reasoning: Adding tagged items changes both the tagged numerator and total denominator. Percentage points subtract shares; relative change divides by the starting share.
acceptableResponses: Equivalent exact fractions or sensibly rounded percentages with all comparison labels
commonErrors: Using the new share as percent-change denominator

criteria.description: Applies the same factor to both terms and obtains 35.
Divides the change by the old value and obtains 25%.
Uses the old 50% reference to obtain a 30% decrease.
Finds and simplifies the initial ordered ratio.
Uses totals 80 and 96 for the two shares.
Labels about 11.67 points and 38.89% relative increase correctly.

sources.title: NIST Guide to the SI, Chapter 8: Comments on Some Quantities and Their Units
creators: National Institute of Standards and Technology
sources.publisher: National Institute of Standards and Technology
sources.locator: Sections 8.4 and 8.6, especially definitions of ratios, fractions, and quotient quantities of dimension one
sources.license: NIST Technical Series/public information under NIST Copyrights & Disclaimers and technical-series licensing statements, subject to attribution and third-party exceptions

uses.rightsBasisEvidence: Only factual quotient and dimension-one relationships are used. All explanations, datasets, examples, prompts, and assessments are original; no NIST expression, table, or image is reproduced.

agentAccess.notes: NIST rights and robots routes were checked before substantive public-page access; no source-specific agent restriction, login, paywall, or access-control barrier was found.

claims.statement: A ratio compares ordered quantities by division; several scientific ratio and fraction quantities are defined explicitly as quotients.
claims.scope: Ordered ratios and quotient quantities in introductory quantitative reasoning.
claims.uncertainty: Whether a proportional model applies to changing quantities requires a separate contextual assumption.

claims.statement: A fraction formed as a ratio of like-dimension quantities is a quantity of dimension one, enabling equivalent fractional, decimal, and percent representations.
claims.scope: Part-whole and change ratios whose numerator and denominator have compatible dimensions.
claims.uncertainty: The word percentage is incomplete without a clearly named reference quantity and comparison type.

terms.definition: An ordered comparison of two quantities by division.

An equation asserting that two ratios are equal, for example a/b = c/d. This is distinct from the statistical sense of 'proportion' as a part-to-whole fraction between 0 and 1, introduced in later data-analysis lessons.
A ratio expressed per hundred relative to a named whole or reference quantity.
The change divided by the starting value, expressed as a percentage and labeled as an increase or decrease.
The arithmetic difference between two percentages, distinct from their relative percent change.
The whole, baseline, or starting value used as the denominator that gives a ratio or percentage its meaning.

Originality, sources, and rights

The reference-first structure, archive opening, queue example, pass-fraction misconception, bead transfer, all values, explanations, feedback, recovery routes, and assessment content are original. All settings are fictional. NIST Chapter 8 is used only as factual authority for quotient-defined ratios and fractions of dimension one. No NIST expression, table, image, or sequence is copied or adapted.

NIST rights, technical-series licensing, and public robots routes were checked before access on 2026-07-22. No agent restriction, login, paywall, or access-control barrier was found. No third-party assets, datasets, personal data, or patient material are included.

This mathematical lesson provides no clinical advice and makes no claim of review, approval, publication, measured effectiveness, credit, or competence.
